message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Viral diversity presents an ongoing challenge for diagnostic tests, which need to accurately detect all circulating variants. The Abbott Global Surveillance program monitors severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) variants and their impact on diagnostic test performance.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the capacity of Abbott molecular, antigen, and serologic assays to detect the SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7, B.1.351 and the P.1 variants.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Virus variant culture stock dilutions (B.1.1.7, BEI NR-54011; B.1.351, BEI NR-54008 and 54009; P.1, BEI NR-54982) and clinical samples from patients with confirmed B.1.1.7 variant infection were run on the Abbott ID NOW COVID-19,
                    <jats:italic>m</jats:italic>
                    2000 RealTi
                    <jats:italic>m</jats:italic>
                    e SARS-CoV-2, Alinity m SARS-CoV-2, and Alinity m Resp-4-Plex molecular assays; the BinaxNOW COVID-19 Ag Card and Panbio COVID-19 Ag Rapid Test Device; and the ARCHITECT/Alinity i SARS-CoV-2 IgG and AdviseDx IgM assays, Panbio COVID-19 IgG assay, and ARCHITECT/Alinity i AdviseDx SARS-CoV-2 IgG II assay.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Cultured virus stocks and B.1.1.7 clinical samples were detected with molecular, antigen, and serologic assays in the expected ranges, confirming
                    <jats:italic>in silico</jats:italic>
                    predictions. The ratio between genome equivalents (GE) and calculated median tissue culture infectious dose (TCID
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    ) were 31-to 83-fold higher for B.1.1.7 cultures compared to B.1.351 and P.1 cultures, demonstrating that GE are more consistent units between cultures than TCID
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Abbott molecular and antigen assays effectively detect B.1.1.7, B.1.351, and P.1 variant infections and Abbott serologic assays detect B.1.1.7 antibodies in patient sera. Future studies with SARS-CoV-2 virus cultures should use quantitative viral load values to compare detection of variants.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Abbott SARS-CoV-2 molecular and antigen assays detect B.1.1.7, B.1.351, and P.1 variants</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Abbott SARS-CoV-2 antibody assays detect B.1.1.7 antibodies in recovered patient sera</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Quantitation of viral load in genome equivalents allows comparison of assay performance</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Detection of SARS-CoV-2 variants by Abbott molecular, antigen, and serological tests
